text: Energy cost (kcal/km) increases with running speed, whereas oxygen cost (L/km) remains unchanged across submaximal speeds below lactate turn point, making oxygen cost an invalid measure of true running economy.
specific_value: OC unchanged across speeds (P = 0.54); EC increased (P < 0.001)

text: Running economy should be measured using energy cost (kcal/km) rather than oxygen cost (L/km), because oxygen cost fails to account for variations in substrate utilization (changes in RER) at different speeds.
Respiratory exchange ratio (RER) increases in a stepwise manner with increments in submaximal running speed, indicating a shift toward greater carbohydrate oxidation at higher intensities.
Linear ratio scaling (dividing by body mass) is more appropriate than power function scaling for removing the influence of body mass when comparing running economy between individuals.
specific_value: EC ratio vs power R: males 0.589 vs 0.588, females 0.498 vs 0.482

text: Valid interindividual comparisons of running economy require expressing energy cost relative to body mass (kcal/kg/km) rather than using absolute values.
Submaximal running economy assessment should use discontinuous stages of approximately 3 minutes with 1 km/h increments at speeds below the lactate turn point to ensure steady-state measurements.
